name: Glass Butte Recreational Rockhound Area
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding area near the Oregon high desert featuring abundant black obsidian, agate, and chalcedony specimens. No permit required for personal use collecting.
description:
Glass Butte is one of Oregon's premier public rockhounding destinations, located on BLM-managed land in the arid Lake County region. The site sits at approximately 6400 feet elevation and is known for producing high-quality black obsidian, including specimens with attractive glassy fractures and sharp edges prized by collectors and knappers. The surrounding terrain features exposed volcanic formations with scattered agate and chalcedony nodules accessible through hiking and surface collecting.

The area benefits from minimal restrictions and no permit requirements, making it ideal for casual rockhounds and serious collectors alike. Unlike many popular sites in Oregon, Glass Butte remains relatively uncrowded due to its remote location and requires basic backcountry travel skills. The volcanic geology here dates to Miocene-era rhyolitic eruptions that deposited extensive obsidian flows and created the silica-rich host rock now weathered to reveal countless collectible specimens.

Visitors should come prepared for high desert conditions with variable weather and limited services. The elevation and exposure create temperature swings from pleasant spring and fall days to harsh winter cold and intense summer sun. Early morning collecting is recommended to avoid afternoon heat and to maximize visibility of dark obsidian specimens against the landscape.
history: Glass Butte's obsidian deposits formed during the Miocene epoch (approximately 20-10 million years ago) when extensive rhyolitic volcanism blanketed the Oregon high desert. The silica-rich lava flows cooled rapidly, creating the obsidian and associated chalcedony deposits visible today. This same volcanic activity created much of the obsidian wealth that made Oregon a rockhounding destination and attracted Native American populations who used obsidian for tools and trade.
tips: Search the exposed hillsides and wash cuts where erosion has revealed fresh obsidian and agate specimens - avoid disturbing stable rock faces. Black obsidian is easier to spot on light-colored pumice and ash, so focus on areas where volcanic layers are clearly visible. Bring a loupe to examine the edge quality and clarity of obsidian specimens before pocketing them, as many pieces show flow patterns or inclusions that affect collectibility. The high desert sun reflects off obsidian surfaces, so wear sunglasses to reduce eye strain and increase visual scanning effectiveness across the rocky terrain.
terrain_notes: Elevation 6397 feet with exposed volcanic ridges, gently rolling high desert terrain interspersed with sagebrush and juniper. Rocky, uneven footing throughout the collecting area with scattered obsidian fragments and sharp volcanic rock. Parking area sits on a dirt pullout with approximately one-quarter mile of easy walking to main collecting zones, extending to moderate hikes of 1-2 miles across open terrain for exploring secondary sites. No shade available; full sun exposure throughout.
best_season: May - September with peak conditions in June and July. Winter snowfall can block access and make the high elevation hazardous. Spring (May-June) and early fall (August-September) offer the best balance of mild temperatures and clear visibility. Summer can bring intense midday heat and afternoon thunderstorms typical of the region.
written_directions: From Lakeview, Oregon (the nearest significant town), take Highway 395 north approximately 20 miles toward Summer Lake. Turn east onto Forest Road 27 (Glass Butte Road) and follow signs for the Glass Butte Rockhounding Area. The final 8-10 miles are on rough dirt/gravel road suitable for high-clearance vehicles. From the main parking pullout, the primary collecting areas are visible on the exposed slopes immediately adjacent to the road and accessible via short hiking trails marked by cairns.
road_conditions: Rough gravel and dirt Forest Road 27 with washboard texture and rocky patches. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Road may be impassable in winter or after heavy rain. Final approach is on a well-maintained dirt parking area.
parking_notes: Primitive dirt pullout with space for approximately 5-8 vehicles. No facilities, no water, no toilets. Parking is first-come, first-served with no designated fee area.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or power tools. Collection limited to surface specimens and hand-gathered material. Remove only what you will use; leave the site in natural condition.
rules:
Personal use only - no commercial collection
Maximum 25 pounds per day limit
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
No power tools or blasting
Collect surface specimens only
Leave no trash - pack out what you pack in
Respect the natural landscape
nearest_services: Lakeview, Oregon - approximately 20-25 miles west. Limited services (one small grocery, gas station, basic restaurants). Larger services (hospitals, major grocery) require travel to Bend or Klamath Falls (1.5-2 hours).
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky footing and scattered sharp obsidian fragments. Hiking required across exposed high desert landscape with no shade or shelter. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots essential due to sharp volcanic rock. The site is best suited for visitors with moderate fitness level comfortable walking 1-2 miles across uneven terrain at 6400+ feet elevation.

faq.question: What kind of obsidian can I find at Glass Butte Rockhounding Area in Oregon?
faq.answer: Glass Butte is famous for high-quality black obsidian with excellent glassy fractures, including mahogany obsidian and specimens suitable for knapping. You will also find scattered agate, chalcedony, and chert nodules across the volcanic terrain. Most collectors find 10-25 quality specimens per visit within the 25 lb daily limit.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Glass Butte near Lake County, Oregon?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Glass Butte. It is on BLM-managed public land where personal use collection is allowed under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act. You must follow the 25 lb per day limit and collect only for personal use - commercial collection is prohibited.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound for obsidian at Glass Butte, Oregon?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding season is May through September, with peak conditions in June and July. Winter snow and cold temperatures make access difficult at this 6400-foot elevation. Spring and early fall offer ideal temperatures and clear visibility for spotting black obsidian against the light volcanic terrain.

faq.question: How difficult is the terrain for beginners rockhounding at Glass Butte Recreational Area?
faq.answer: Glass Butte is beginner-friendly for adults and children 8 and older, though it requires moderate hiking across uneven volcanic terrain with sharp rocks. The main collecting area is accessible within one-quarter mile of parking. Sturdy hiking boots are essential, and full sun exposure means bringing water and sun protection. Families with young children should stay close to the parking area where easiest specimens are found.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring for rockhounding at Glass Butte, Oregon?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots (sharp obsidian fragments are common), thick gloves to protect hands from sharp edges, a hand lens or loupe to evaluate specimen quality, a small hand pick or chisel for extracting specimens, a backpack, sunscreen, sunglasses, at least 2 liters of water per person, and a five-gallon bucket for carrying specimens. A headlamp is useful for early morning collection before the sun creates glare.

faq.question: How do I get to Glass Butte Rockhounding Area from Lakeview and what roads are required?
faq.answer: From Lakeview, take Highway 395 north approximately 20 miles toward Summer Lake, then turn east onto Forest Road 27 (Glass Butte Road). Follow the rough gravel and dirt road 8-10 miles to the main parking pullout. High-clearance vehicles (AWD or 4x4) are recommended due to washboard and rocky road conditions. The road may be impassable in winter or after heavy rain.

meta_title: Glass Butte Rockhounding - Free Oregon Obsidian Area
meta_description: Find black obsidian, agate, and chalcedony at Glass Butte in Oregon. Free BLM rockhounding, no permit, 25 lb daily limit. Moderate terrain, best May-September.
